stem: A four-month-old has been on humidified high-flow nasal cannula at 2 L/kg/min with FiO2 0.40 for four hours for RSV bronchiolitis. Mother says he breastfed only briefly before tiring. On examination there is marked substernal recession, head bob, and audible grunting. Capillary blood gas: pH 7.28, pCO2 58 mmHg, HCO3 22 mmol/L, lactate 1.4 mmol/L. Chest X-ray shows hyperinflation with no focal consolidation. The paediatric retrieval team has been contacted.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 178, BP 78/45 mmHg, RR 68, SpO2 89%, temperature 37.9°C
data_table.Examination: Substernal recession, head bob, grunting, lethargic but rousable
data_table.Investigations: Capillary blood gas pH 7.28, pCO2 58 mmHg, HCO3 22 mmol/L, lactate 1.4 mmol/L; chest X-ray hyperinflated, no focal consolidation
data_table.Medications: Humidified HFNC 2 L/kg/min, FiO2 0.40 for 4 hours

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Initiate CPAP at 6 cm H2O via retrieval team prior to PICU transfer
options.rationale: Rising pCO2 with persistent severe work of breathing on the maximal RCH HFNC ceiling defines HFNC failure. CPAP is the next non-invasive escalation, started during retrieval and continued in PICU. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Endotracheal intubation and conventional ventilation immediately
options.rationale: Intubation is reserved for apnoea, exhaustion, or CPAP failure. With lactate 1.4 mmol/L and no shock, a CPAP trial precedes intubation. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Increase HFNC flow to 3 L/kg/min and reassess after one hour
options.rationale: RCH ceiling for HFNC in bronchiolitis is 2 L/kg/min. Further flow does not improve outcomes and delays appropriate escalation. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg with hypertonic saline 3% nebuliser
options.rationale: Salbutamol and hypertonic saline are not recommended for routine bronchiolitis under 12 months in Australia and do not reverse respiratory failure. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg empirically for bacterial co-infection
options.rationale: Bacterial co-infection is uncommon in bronchiolitis. With a clear viral cause and no focal X-ray finding, empirical antibiotics are not indicated. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

explanation.summary: This infant meets HFNC-failure criteria: persistent severe work of breathing with rising pCO2 (58 mmHg) and SpO2 below the RCH target of 90% on maximal flow. The correct response is non-invasive escalation to CPAP, ideally during PICU retrieval. Intubation is held back for apnoea, GCS drop, or CPAP failure. Increasing HFNC above 2 L/kg/min has no evidence base and delays appropriate escalation.
key_points: RCH bronchiolitis HFNC ceiling is 2 L/kg/min
HFNC failure criteria: persistent severe WOB, rising pCO2, SpO2 below 90% target
CPAP precedes intubation when no apnoea or shock
explanation.pearls: If pCO2 rises above 55 mmHg on maximal HFNC, escalate non-invasively within 30 minutes; intubate for apnoea or GCS drop, not for the gas number alone.

stem: A five-week-old boy presents with two weeks of forceful non-bilious vomiting after every feed. Mother describes the vomiting as 'shooting across the cot'. He looks sunken-eyed and his nappies have been very dry. Ultrasound shows pyloric muscle thickness 4.5 mm and channel length 18 mm. Surgical team plans pyloromyotomy. Venous gas: pH 7.52, pCO2 48 mmHg, HCO3 36 mmol/L, sodium 132 mmol/L, potassium 2.9 mmol/L, chloride 88 mmol/L.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 168, BP 82/48 mmHg, RR 38, SpO2 99%, temperature 36.7°C
data_table.Examination: Sunken anterior fontanelle, dry mucous membranes, weight 3.8 kg (was 4.4 kg)
data_table.Investigations: VBG pH 7.52, pCO2 48 mmHg, HCO3 36 mmol/L, Na 132, K 2.9, Cl 88 mmol/L; ultrasound pylorus 4.5 mm thick, channel 18 mm

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Resuscitate with 0.45% saline plus 5% dextrose plus 20 mmol/L KCl
options.rationale: Hypotonic fluid worsens the hyponatraemia and is contraindicated for paediatric resuscitation in Australia. (Source: ANZICS paediatric fluid statement 2020)

options.text: Correct alkalosis and electrolytes with 0.9% saline plus 20 mmol/L KCl before theatre
options.rationale: Pyloric stenosis with hypochloraemic hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis requires correction (Cl above 100 mmol/L, HCO3 below 28 mmol/L, K above 3.5 mmol/L) before anaesthesia. Isotonic saline with added potassium is the RCH standard. (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric stenosis)

options.text: Proceed directly to pyloromyotomy after one 20 mL/kg saline bolus
options.rationale: Anaesthetic apnoea is prolonged by uncorrected metabolic alkalosis. Surgery is delayed until biochemistry is corrected. (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric stenosis)

options.text: Start IV omeprazole and nasogastric feeding to bypass the pylorus
options.rationale: Pyloric stenosis is mechanical; acid suppression and NG feeding do not address obstruction. (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric stenosis)

options.text: Give 4% albumin 20 mL/kg bolus then transfer urgently for theatre
options.rationale: Colloid is not first-line; isotonic crystalloid with potassium addresses the specific alkalosis. (Source: ANZICS paediatric fluid statement 2020)

explanation.summary: Infantile pyloric stenosis produces a hypochloraemic hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis from prolonged vomiting of gastric HCl. Anaesthesia before correction risks prolonged apnoea and arrhythmia. RCH targets chloride above 100 mmol/L, bicarbonate below 28 mmol/L, potassium above 3.5 mmol/L, and adequate urine output before theatre. Isotonic saline with added potassium achieves all three; hypotonic fluid worsens the hyponatraemia.
key_points: Resuscitation targets: Cl above 100, HCO3 below 28, K above 3.5 mmol/L
0.9% saline plus 20 mmol/L KCl is RCH standard
Hypotonic maintenance fluid is contraindicated in paediatric resuscitation
explanation.pearls: Surgery for pyloric stenosis is never an emergency; biochemistry first, scalpel second.

stem: An eight-year-old known asthmatic presents with worsening dyspnoea over six hours. He has had three back-to-back salbutamol nebulisers with ipratropium and oral prednisolone 2 mg/kg in ED. Forty minutes after the third nebuliser he still has marked subcostal recession, a silent left lower zone, and is speaking in single words. Pulse rate 140, blood pressure 102/68 mmHg, respiratory rate 36, SpO2 91% on 6 L/min via Hudson mask, temperature 36.9°C. Peak flow cannot be obtained. Venous gas: pH 7.32, pCO2 46 mmHg, HCO3 23 mmol/L, lactate 3.8 mmol/L.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 140, BP 102/68 mmHg, RR 36, SpO2 91% on 6 L Hudson, temperature 36.9°C
data_table.Examination: Speaks single words, marked recession, silent LLZ
data_table.Investigations: VBG pH 7.32, pCO2 46 mmHg, HCO3 23 mmol/L, lactate 3.8 mmol/L
data_table.Medications: Salbutamol 5 mg nebs x3 with ipratropium 250 mcg x3; prednisolone 2 mg/kg PO

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continuous salbutamol nebulisation at 0.5 mg/kg/hour for 60 minutes
options.rationale: He has had maximal intermittent nebulisation without response and tachycardia plus lactate above 3 mmol/L indicate beta-agonist toxicity. Further salbutamol without IV magnesium is unlikely to help. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma)

options.text: IV aminophylline 5 mg/kg loading over 20 minutes then infusion
options.rationale: Aminophylline is third-line after IV magnesium has failed and carries arrhythmia and seizure risk. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma)

options.text: IV magnesium sulfate 50 mg/kg over 20 minutes with cardiac monitoring
options.rationale: Failure to respond to maximal first-line bronchodilator and steroid in severe asthma is the trigger for IV magnesium. RCH dose is 50 mg/kg (max 2 g) over 20 minutes with cardiac monitoring. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma)

options.text: IV hydrocortisone 4 mg/kg as he has had only oral steroid
options.rationale: Oral prednisolone is equivalent to IV hydrocortisone for asthma; switching does not bridge ongoing bronchospasm. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma)

options.text: Intubate and ventilate with ketamine induction in resus bay
options.rationale: Intubation in severe asthma is high-risk and reserved for arrest, exhaustion, or persistent hypoxia despite second-line therapy. IV magnesium precedes it. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma)

explanation.summary: Failure to respond to three back-to-back salbutamol-ipratropium nebulisers plus systemic steroid in a child with severe asthma triggers IV magnesium sulfate. RCH dose is 50 mg/kg (max 2 g) over 20 minutes with cardiac monitoring. Aminophylline is third-line. Intubation is reserved for exhaustion, arrest, or persistent hypoxia despite second-line therapy.
key_points: IV magnesium 50 mg/kg over 20 minutes is RCH second-line in severe asthma
Steroid route does not matter for asthma; PO equals IV
Lactate above 3 mmol/L in salbutamol-treated asthma usually reflects beta-agonist effect, not sepsis
explanation.pearls: If the child is still working hard 30 to 60 minutes after the third bronchodilator burst, give IV magnesium - do not keep nebulising in hope.

stem: A nine-week-old presents to ED with father who reports the baby 'rolled off the change table' onto carpet 90 minutes ago. The baby has not yet rolled developmentally and was 'a bit floppy' afterwards but is now feeding. Examination shows a torn upper lip frenulum, a 2 cm boggy left parietal swelling, and three small round bruises on the left cheek and right upper arm. Fundoscopy reveals bilateral pre-retinal haemorrhages. CT brain shows a thin left subdural haematoma and an associated parietal skull fracture. The father is distressed and asks to leave with the baby to return tomorrow.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 158, BP 78/46 mmHg, RR 42, SpO2 99%, temperature 36.8°C
data_table.Examination: Torn upper lip frenulum, left parietal boggy swelling 2 cm, three small round bruises (cheek and right upper arm), AF soft and flat
data_table.Investigations: CT brain: thin left subdural haematoma plus parietal skull fracture; bilateral pre-retinal haemorrhages on fundoscopy

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate action?

options.text: Discharge home with parents after social work phone follow-up tomorrow
options.rationale: The injury pattern is medically inconsistent with the history and meets mandatory reporting thresholds for serious physical abuse. Discharging is unsafe. (Source: RCH CPG: Child abuse)

options.text: Arrange outpatient MRI brain and request a coagulation screen as priority
options.rationale: Coagulation screen is appropriate but secondary; the immediate priority is protective admission and statutory notification, not outpatient imaging. (Source: RCH CPG: Child abuse)

options.text: Allow father to leave with the baby pending paediatric review next week
options.rationale: Allowing return with the carer leaves the infant at risk. Admission and statutory notification are mandatory. (Source: RCH CPG: Child abuse)

options.text: Admit, notify child protection services, and complete a skeletal survey
options.rationale: Bruising in a pre-mobile infant, torn frenulum, subdural with fracture, and retinal haemorrhages are sentinel injuries. Mandatory reporting, admission for safety, and skeletal survey within 24 hours are the RCH protocol. (Source: RCH CPG: Child abuse)

options.text: Order a metabolic bone disease workup to exclude osteogenesis imperfecta
options.rationale: Differentials are pursued in parallel with protection, not instead of it. Statutory notification cannot wait for metabolic results. (Source: RCH CPG: Child abuse)

explanation.summary: Bruising in a pre-mobile infant, torn frenulum, subdural haematoma with skull fracture from a low-fall history, and bilateral retinal haemorrhages each independently meet sentinel-injury criteria. Mandatory reporting to child protection services, protective admission, and a skeletal survey within 24 hours are all required. Differential workup (coagulation, metabolic bone disease) proceeds in parallel, not instead. The carer is not given the choice to leave.
key_points: Any bruise in a pre-mobile infant warrants child protection consideration
Sentinel injuries: torn frenulum, retinal haemorrhages, posterior rib or metaphyseal fractures
Skeletal survey within 24 hours per RCH protocol
explanation.pearls: If a story does not match physiology (90-minute-old fall but baby cannot yet roll), believe the injuries, not the history.

sources.label: Victorian Children Youth and Families Act 2005

stem: A previously well 19-month-old has a single 90-second generalised tonic-clonic seizure during a febrile URTI. By the time you assess her she is alert, interactive, and feeding normally. She has no focal neurological signs and her anterior fontanelle is closed. Her older brother had two febrile seizures at two years of age. There is no family history of epilepsy. The mother asks whether her daughter needs admission, anti-seizure medication, or imaging.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 148, BP 96/58 mmHg, RR 32, SpO2 99%, temperature 39.1°C
data_table.Examination: Alert, no neck stiffness, no rash, ENT mildly inflamed tympanic membranes bilaterally

lead_in: What is the most appropriate disposition and counselling?

options.text: Admit overnight and start prophylactic oral diazepam for the next febrile illness
options.rationale: Prophylactic benzodiazepines for febrile seizures are not recommended; recurrence does not predict epilepsy. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile seizure)

options.text: Arrange outpatient EEG and brain MRI before allowing return home
options.rationale: EEG and imaging are not indicated for a simple febrile seizure with normal neurology. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile seizure)

options.text: Admit for 24 hours of paracetamol every 4 hours to prevent further fever spikes
options.rationale: Regular antipyretics do not reduce recurrence and admission is not required for a simple febrile seizure. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile seizure)

options.text: Perform a lumbar puncture before any decision is taken on disposition
options.rationale: LP is not routine in a well child over 12 months without meningism, prolonged post-ictal state, or sepsis features. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile seizure)

options.text: Discharge with parental advice on first aid and risk of one-in-three recurrence
options.rationale: Simple febrile seizure with a normal post-ictal child and no red flags is managed by discharge with first-aid education and accurate recurrence quotation (around 30%). (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile seizure)

explanation.summary: A simple febrile seizure is generalised, lasts under 15 minutes, does not recur within 24 hours, and occurs in a 6-month to 5-year-old with no focal deficit. Imaging, EEG, and admission are not indicated when the child is well post-ictally and no sepsis or meningitis features are present. Parents are counselled on first aid, the roughly one-in-three recurrence risk, and the small absolute increase in lifetime epilepsy risk (about 1% above baseline 1%). Antipyretics treat discomfort but do not prevent recurrence.
key_points: Simple febrile seizure: generalised, under 15 minutes, single in 24 hours, age 6 months to 5 years
Recurrence about 30%; lifetime epilepsy risk only about 2% absolute
No imaging, EEG, prophylaxis, or routine LP if child well over 12 months
explanation.pearls: Antipyretics make a hot child feel better; they do not prevent febrile seizures.

stem: A 14-year-old presents with three weeks of fatigue, scleral icterus, and new tremor that the school noticed in handwriting class. She has not been on any new medications and there is no travel or paracetamol exposure. Her mother died of liver failure at 38. On examination she has hepatomegaly, dysarthria, and dark coloured urine. Bloods: haemoglobin 84 g/L (115-155), reticulocytes 6%, bilirubin 78 micromol/L (5-20), ALT 220 U/L (5-40), AST 290 U/L, ALP only 32 U/L (115-300), INR 1.6, ceruloplasmin 0.07 g/L (0.20-0.60), direct antiglobulin test negative.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 96, BP 112/64 mmHg, RR 18, SpO2 98%, temperature 36.7°C
data_table.Examination: Scleral icterus, hepatomegaly 3 cm, dysarthria, intention tremor on finger-nose; Kayser-Fleischer rings on slit lamp
data_table.Investigations: Hb 84 g/L, retics 6%, bilirubin 78 micromol/L, ALT 220 U/L, AST 290 U/L, ALP 32 U/L, INR 1.6, ceruloplasmin 0.07 g/L, DAT negative, 24-hour urinary copper pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Perform 24-hour urinary copper and arrange urgent percutaneous liver biopsy
options.rationale: Low ceruloplasmin, KF rings, Coombs-negative haemolysis, neuropsychiatric signs, and ALP:bilirubin ratio below 4 meet Wilson disease criteria. 24-hour urinary copper plus liver biopsy for hepatic copper quantification confirm diagnosis and guide chelation. (Source: AASLD Wilson disease guidance 2022)

options.text: Send peripheral blood film and direct antiglobulin testing for cold agglutinins
options.rationale: A negative DAT plus very low ALP and dysarthria with KF rings makes immune haemolysis far less likely than Wilson disease. (Source: AASLD Wilson disease guidance 2022)

options.text: Repeat full blood count and reticulocyte count in 72 hours to confirm trend
options.rationale: Watching the trend delays definitive diagnosis and risks fulminant hepatic failure. The clinical picture already strongly suggests Wilson disease. (Source: AASLD Wilson disease guidance 2022)

options.text: Order MRI brain with susceptibility imaging for basal ganglia copper deposits
options.rationale: MRI may show basal ganglia changes but does not establish the diagnosis or guide chelation; biochemical and biopsy confirmation come first. (Source: AASLD Wilson disease guidance 2022)

options.text: Send paracetamol level, alpha-1 antitrypsin, and autoimmune hepatitis panel
options.rationale: These differentials are part of acute liver failure workup but do not explain the Coombs-negative haemolysis and KF rings; Wilson confirmation cannot wait. (Source: AASLD Wilson disease guidance 2022)

explanation.summary: An adolescent with Coombs-negative haemolytic anaemia, low alkaline phosphatase relative to a high bilirubin (ALP:bilirubin ratio under 4 strongly supports Wilson), neuropsychiatric tremor, KF rings, and very low ceruloplasmin should be worked up for fulminant Wilsonian crisis. Twenty-four-hour urinary copper above 1.6 micromol/24 hours and hepatic copper above 250 micrograms/g dry weight on biopsy confirm the diagnosis and direct urgent chelation (or transplant referral if INR continues to rise).
key_points: ALP:bilirubin ratio under 4 in acute liver failure suggests Wilson
Coombs-negative haemolysis plus liver failure should prompt copper workup
Ceruloplasmin below 0.10 g/L plus KF rings has high specificity in adolescents
explanation.pearls: In an adolescent with liver failure and a tremor, always order ceruloplasmin and slit-lamp examination before settling on autoimmune hepatitis.

stem: A six-year-old boy presents with two days of refusal to weight bear on his right leg following a viral URTI last week. He has had a low-grade fever overnight. He holds the right hip flexed, abducted, and externally rotated, and resists log-roll on the right. Pulse rate 116, blood pressure 102/64 mmHg, respiratory rate 22, SpO2 99%, temperature 38.7°C. Bloods: WCC 14.5 x10^9/L, CRP 64 mg/L, ESR 48 mm/hr. Ultrasound shows a moderate right hip joint effusion.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 116, BP 102/64 mmHg, RR 22, SpO2 99%, temperature 38.7°C
data_table.Examination: Right hip held in flexion, abduction, external rotation; refuses weight bearing; log-roll resisted
data_table.Investigations: WCC 14.5 x10^9/L, neutrophils 11.0, CRP 64 mg/L, ESR 48 mm/hr; ultrasound right hip moderate effusion; blood cultures pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Discharge with rest and ibuprofen and review in 48 hours for transient synovitis
options.rationale: All four Kocher predictors are met (fever above 38.5°C, refusal to weight bear, ESR above 40 mm/hr, WCC above 12 x10^9/L), making septic arthritis far more likely than transient synovitis. (Source: RCH CPG: Limp)

options.text: Refer for theatre aspiration and washout with IV flucloxacillin after cultures
options.rationale: With all four Kocher predictors met the probability of septic arthritis is around 99%. Urgent joint aspiration plus washout and empirical IV flucloxacillin 50 mg/kg q6h (covering S. aureus and S. pyogenes) is the RCH pathway. (Source: RCH CPG: Septic arthritis)

options.text: Arrange MRI hip with contrast before any decision on aspiration is taken
options.rationale: MRI can clarify osteomyelitis but should not delay theatre when Kocher criteria are met. (Source: RCH CPG: Septic arthritis)

options.text: Admit for serial examination and repeat inflammatory markers in 24 hours
options.rationale: Delaying joint aspiration risks irreversible cartilage damage in confirmed septic arthritis. (Source: RCH CPG: Septic arthritis)

options.text: Start oral cefalexin 25 mg/kg q6h and arrange outpatient orthopaedic review
options.rationale: Oral antibiotics and outpatient review are inappropriate for likely septic arthritis. (Source: RCH CPG: Septic arthritis)

explanation.summary: Kocher criteria for paediatric septic hip identify four predictors: fever above 38.5°C, non-weight-bearing, ESR above 40 mm/hr, and WCC above 12 x10^9/L. Meeting all four gives roughly 99% probability of septic arthritis. The RCH pathway is urgent theatre for aspiration plus washout and empirical IV flucloxacillin after blood cultures, with cover broadened for at-risk groups (e.g. neonates, immunocompromised, Kingella suspicion). MRI clarifies osteomyelitis but should not delay washout.
key_points: All four Kocher predictors: septic arthritis probability about 99%
Empirical IV flucloxacillin 50 mg/kg q6h covers S. aureus and S. pyogenes
Theatre and aspiration precede MRI when Kocher is positive
explanation.pearls: A child who refuses to weight bear with raised CRP and a hip effusion goes to theatre, not radiology, until septic arthritis is excluded by aspirate.

stem: A 31-year-old primigravida at 32+2 weeks presents with new severe headache and right upper quadrant pain. Booking blood pressure was 110/70 mmHg. Today her blood pressure is 168/112 mmHg after 30 minutes of rest. She has had three doses of oral nifedipine 20 mg over 90 minutes with blood pressure now 152/98 mmHg. CTG is reassuring. Bloods: platelets 92 x10^9/L (150-400), ALT 96 U/L (5-35), creatinine 96 micromol/L (45-90), urine protein-creatinine ratio 86 mg/mmol. Foetal growth was at the 25th centile two weeks ago.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 96, BP 152/98 mmHg, RR 18, SpO2 99%, temperature 36.8°C, FHR 140
data_table.Examination: Brisk reflexes, no clonus, mild RUQ tenderness, no peripheral oedema documented
data_table.Investigations: Platelets 92, ALT 96 U/L, creatinine 96 micromol/L, urate 0.42 mmol/L, urine PCR 86 mg/mmol; CTG normal variability with accelerations
data_table.Medications: Nifedipine immediate release 20 mg PO x3 over 90 minutes

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Expectant management until 37 weeks with twice-weekly bloods, CTG and growth scan
options.rationale: Expectant management beyond 34 weeks is not appropriate with severe disease and falling platelets; delivery within 24-48 hours is indicated. (Source: SOMANZ hypertension in pregnancy 2014)

options.text: Discharge home on oral labetalol 200 mg three times daily with weekly review
options.rationale: Severe pre-eclampsia is a hospital diagnosis; discharge with significant end-organ involvement is unsafe. (Source: SOMANZ hypertension in pregnancy 2014)

options.text: Plan delivery within 24-48 hours after IV magnesium and antenatal corticosteroids
options.rationale: Severe pre-eclampsia with hepatic and renal involvement and falling platelets at 32 weeks requires planned delivery within 24-48 hours. Betamethasone 11.4 mg IM x2 (24 hours apart) and IV magnesium sulfate for neuroprotection precede delivery. (Source: SOMANZ hypertension in pregnancy 2014)

options.text: Proceed to immediate emergency caesarean before any steroid loading dose
options.rationale: With reassuring CTG and controlled BP, a 24-48 hour window allows steroid benefit; immediate delivery is reserved for foetal compromise or maternal instability. (Source: SOMANZ hypertension in pregnancy 2014)

options.text: Continue oral nifedipine and add aspirin 100 mg daily for preventive effect
options.rationale: Aspirin prevents pre-eclampsia from the first trimester; it has no role at 32 weeks with established severe disease. (Source: SOMANZ hypertension in pregnancy 2014)

explanation.summary: Severe pre-eclampsia with hepatic transaminitis, renal involvement, and a platelet count under 100 x10^9/L at 32 weeks indicates delivery within 24-48 hours per SOMANZ. The window allows antenatal corticosteroids for foetal lung maturity and IV magnesium for neuroprotection. Immediate caesarean is reserved for non-reassuring CTG or maternal instability. Expectant management is not appropriate once severe features are established.
key_points: Severe pre-eclampsia from 32 weeks: deliver within 24-48 hours after steroids
IV magnesium sulfate is neuroprotective and reduces eclampsia
Aspirin prevents pre-eclampsia from early pregnancy; not a rescue therapy
explanation.pearls: When severe pre-eclampsia is diagnosed remote from term, the question is how to deliver safely, not whether to deliver.

stem: A 34-year-old G2P1 has had a normal vaginal delivery of a 4.1 kg infant after a four-hour second stage. Active management of the third stage was performed with oxytocin 10 IU IM. Twenty minutes after the placenta the uterine fundus is boggy at the umbilicus and 900 mL of blood has been lost. She has been catheterised, the placenta is reported as complete, no genital tract trauma is identified, and there are no clots in the vagina. She had blood pressure 158/104 mmHg in late pregnancy and is on oral labetalol 200 mg three times daily. Current blood pressure is 132/88 mmHg.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 108, BP 132/88 mmHg, RR 20, SpO2 99%, temperature 36.9°C
data_table.Examination: Fundus boggy at umbilicus, ongoing trickle PV, perineum intact, placenta reported complete
data_table.Investigations: Estimated blood loss 900 mL and rising; Hb pending
data_table.Medications: Oxytocin 10 IU IM (third stage), labetalol 200 mg TDS PO, IV access secured with 1 L Hartmann's running

lead_in: Which uterotonic is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: IM ergometrine 250 micrograms with prochlorperazine 12.5 mg for nausea
options.rationale: Ergometrine is contraindicated in any current or past hypertensive disorder of pregnancy due to severe vasoconstriction risk. (Source: RANZCOG PPH 2022)

options.text: Misoprostol 800 micrograms sublingual after one further oxytocin infusion bolus
options.rationale: Misoprostol is third-line in Australia after a parenteral prostaglandin (carboprost), particularly when IV/IM access is available. (Source: RANZCOG PPH 2022)

options.text: IV tranexamic acid 1 g over 10 minutes as the sole pharmacological action
options.rationale: Tranexamic acid is adjunctive and given within 3 hours, but it does not replace a second-line uterotonic when the uterus is atonic. (Source: RANZCOG PPH 2022)

options.text: IM carboprost 250 micrograms while preparing for theatre balloon tamponade
options.rationale: Carboprost (a prostaglandin F2-alpha analogue) is the appropriate second-line uterotonic when ergometrine is contraindicated. It is given IM 250 micrograms, repeatable to 2 mg total, with theatre escalation prepared in parallel. (Source: RANZCOG PPH 2022)

options.text: IV magnesium sulfate 4 g bolus to relax associated arterial spasm and clot
options.rationale: Magnesium is uterine-relaxing and worsens atony; it has no role in active PPH from atony. (Source: RANZCOG PPH 2022)

explanation.summary: Atonic PPH not responding to oxytocin and uterine massage requires a second-line uterotonic. Ergometrine is contraindicated with any current or past hypertensive disorder. The RANZCOG alternative is IM carboprost 250 micrograms, repeatable every 15 minutes to a maximum 2 mg. Misoprostol sublingual 800 micrograms is third-line. Tranexamic acid 1 g IV is an adjunct within three hours of bleeding onset, not a replacement.
key_points: Ergometrine contraindicated in hypertensive disorders of pregnancy
Carboprost 250 mcg IM is second-line uterotonic when ergometrine cannot be used
Tranexamic acid is adjunctive, not a substitute for uterotonic
explanation.pearls: Boggy uterus plus ongoing bleed plus blood pressure history that rules out ergometrine equals carboprost reflex.

stem: A 28-year-old G1P0 at 39+1 weeks attends the birth suite reporting a 'gush' of clear fluid 14 hours ago. She is not in labour. Vaginal speculum confirms pooling of clear amniotic fluid. The cardiotocograph is reactive. Her group B streptococcus swab at 36 weeks was negative. She has had no fevers and reports normal foetal movements. She tells you she would prefer to go home and 'see if labour starts on its own'.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 84, BP 118/72 mmHg, RR 16, SpO2 99%, temperature 36.7°C, FHR 138
data_table.Examination: Speculum confirms amniotic pooling, no offensive discharge, cervix appears 1 cm dilated visually
data_table.Investigations: GBS negative at 36 weeks; CTG reactive

lead_in: What is the most appropriate recommendation?

options.text: Discharge home with a thermometer and review in 48 hours if not in labour
options.rationale: Australian practice offers expectant management up to about 24 hours, but inpatient observation and induction within 24 hours improves neonatal outcomes versus outpatient waiting beyond that. (Source: RANZCOG term PROM 2017)

options.text: Commence oxytocin infusion now in the birth suite with epidural insertion
options.rationale: Immediate oxytocin without informed consent or analgesia discussion bypasses shared decision making; an interval allowing spontaneous labour is offered. (Source: RANZCOG term PROM 2017)

options.text: Insert a Foley balloon for cervical ripening and start prophylactic ampicillin
options.rationale: Mechanical ripening is not first-line in established membrane rupture and routine ampicillin is not given when GBS swab is negative without fever. (Source: RANZCOG term PROM 2017)

options.text: Recommend caesarean section now for prolonged rupture exceeding 12 hours
options.rationale: Term PROM is not by itself an indication for caesarean. (Source: RANZCOG term PROM 2017)

options.text: Offer induction now or within 24 hours of rupture with inpatient monitoring
options.rationale: Term PROM with GBS-negative status is offered induction by 24 hours from rupture. Inpatient monitoring through that window with induction by syntocinon is the RANZCOG-recommended pathway. (Source: RANZCOG term PROM 2017)

explanation.summary: Term prelabour rupture of membranes is managed by offering induction with oxytocin by 24 hours from rupture, with inpatient observation in the interval. GBS-negative women without fever do not need prophylactic antibiotics at this point. Discharge home for outpatient waiting is not the Australian standard, and immediate caesarean is not indicated. Foley ripening is reserved for unfavourable cervix without ruptured membranes.
key_points: Term PROM: offer induction within 24 hours of rupture
GBS-negative term PROM without fever: no prophylactic antibiotics yet
Inpatient monitoring throughout the latency interval
explanation.pearls: Counsel the patient on both options (induction now or wait up to 24 hours) and document her informed choice.

stem: A 26-year-old G2P1 at 19+4 weeks attends fortnightly ultrasound for a known monochorionic-diamniotic twin pregnancy. Today the recipient twin's deepest vertical pocket is 8.6 cm and the donor twin's deepest vertical pocket is 1.4 cm. Both bladders are visible, umbilical artery Dopplers show normal end diastolic flow in both, and venous Doppler waveforms are normal. Both growth percentiles are on track for gestation. Maternal observations are normal and she reports a tight abdomen but no contractions.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 84, BP 118/72 mmHg, RR 16, SpO2 99%, temperature 36.7°C, no contractions on tocograph
data_table.Investigations: Twin A (recipient) DVP 8.6 cm, bladder visible, normal Dopplers; Twin B (donor) DVP 1.4 cm, bladder visible, normal Dopplers; growth concordant

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Refer urgently to a foetal medicine unit for assessment and laser candidacy
options.rationale: Quintero stage I TTTS (DVP discordance with bladders visible and normal Dopplers) requires urgent referral to a foetal therapy centre. Foetoscopic laser ablation of placental anastomoses is the standard treatment from stage II and is considered case-by-case for stage I. (Source: ISUOG monochorionic twin guideline 2016)

options.text: Continue fortnightly scans and review at the next routine appointment
options.rationale: Quintero stage I TTTS still requires urgent foetal medicine review; routine fortnightly monitoring under local team is not enough. (Source: ISUOG monochorionic twin guideline 2016)

options.text: Perform serial amnioreductions weekly to control polyhydramnios in recipient
options.rationale: Amnioreduction is second-line; laser ablation has better neurological outcomes for stages II to IV. (Source: ISUOG monochorionic twin guideline 2016)

options.text: Plan elective caesarean delivery at 32 weeks after antenatal corticosteroids
options.rationale: Stable monochorionic-diamniotic twins are normally delivered at 36+0 to 36+6 weeks; pre-empting at 32 weeks is not warranted without indication. (Source: ISUOG monochorionic twin guideline 2016)

options.text: Start maternal sildenafil 20 mg three times daily to improve donor perfusion
options.rationale: Sildenafil has no role in TTTS; the STRIDER trial found no benefit and possible foetal harm. (Source: ISUOG monochorionic twin guideline 2016)

explanation.summary: Quintero stage I TTTS is defined by polyhydramnios in the recipient (DVP greater than 8 cm before 20 weeks) and oligohydramnios in the donor (DVP less than 2 cm), with both bladders visible and normal Dopplers. Even stage I requires urgent foetal medicine referral because progression to stages II-IV can be rapid. Foetoscopic laser ablation of placental anastomoses is the standard treatment for stages II-IV and is considered case-by-case for stage I. Amnioreduction is second-line; sildenafil has no role.
key_points: Stage I TTTS: DVP discordance with visible bladders and normal Dopplers
Laser ablation is standard for stages II-IV TTTS
Urgent foetal medicine referral required even at stage I
explanation.pearls: DVP above 8 cm before 20 weeks or above 10 cm from 20 weeks defines polyhydramnios in monochorionic twins; pair with DVP under 2 cm in the co-twin to call TTTS.

sources.label: RANZCOG management of monochorionic twin pregnancy

stem: A 32-year-old G4P0 presents two weeks after her third consecutive first-trimester loss. All three losses occurred between 8 and 11 weeks, two were ultrasound-confirmed missed miscarriages, and one was a complete miscarriage at home. Her menstrual cycles are regular at 28 days. She has no history of thromboembolism, no family history of miscarriage, and no chronic disease. Karyotyping of the most recent products of conception showed 46,XX with no aneuploidy. She is otherwise well and asks what investigations are appropriate now.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 72, BP 118/74 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 99%, temperature 36.7°C
data_table.Examination: Body mass index 24, no acanthosis, no goitre, no hirsutism
data_table.Investigations: Cycle history regular; recent products of conception karyotype 46,XX

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line investigation panel?

options.text: Inherited thrombophilia panel including factor V Leiden and prothrombin gene
options.rationale: Inherited thrombophilia testing is not recommended routinely in recurrent miscarriage without personal or family VTE history. (Source: ESHRE recurrent pregnancy loss 2022)

options.text: Pelvic ultrasound with antiphospholipid screen and thyroid function tests
options.rationale: RANZCOG and ESHRE recommend a structural assessment (pelvic ultrasound or sonohysterogram), lupus anticoagulant plus anticardiolipin plus beta-2-glycoprotein-1 antibodies for antiphospholipid syndrome, and TSH for thyroid disease as first-line in recurrent miscarriage. (Source: ESHRE recurrent pregnancy loss 2022)

options.text: Endometrial biopsy with natural killer cell quantification and HLA typing
options.rationale: Immune cell quantification is not standard of care and is not validated for routine recurrent miscarriage workup. (Source: ESHRE recurrent pregnancy loss 2022)

options.text: Parental karyotyping for both partners and routine sperm DNA fragmentation
options.rationale: Parental karyotyping is not first-line when products of conception have shown normal karyotype; reserved for selected couples after first-line workup. (Source: ESHRE recurrent pregnancy loss 2022)

options.text: Empirical low-dose aspirin and LMWH from positive pregnancy test next cycle
options.rationale: Empirical anticoagulation without confirmed antiphospholipid syndrome does not improve live birth rates and is not recommended. (Source: ESHRE recurrent pregnancy loss 2022)

explanation.summary: Recurrent miscarriage (three or more consecutive first-trimester losses) warrants a structural uterine assessment, antiphospholipid antibody screen, and thyroid function as first-line. Inherited thrombophilia panels, immune assays, and parental karyotyping are reserved for second-line workup in selected cases. Empirical aspirin or LMWH outside antiphospholipid syndrome is not evidence-based and may harm.
key_points: First-line: pelvic ultrasound, APS triple antibody panel, TSH
Inherited thrombophilia testing reserved for personal/family VTE
Empirical anticoagulation outside APS is not recommended
explanation.pearls: APS testing must be repeated 12 weeks apart for confirmation; do not commit to lifelong therapy on a single positive.

stem: A 33-year-old G2P2 was diagnosed with gestational diabetes at 28 weeks during this pregnancy and was managed with diet alone (no insulin or metformin). She has now had an uncomplicated normal vaginal delivery of a healthy 3.6 kg baby at 39+5 weeks. She is six weeks postpartum, breastfeeding, and her finger-prick glucose readings have been 5.6 to 6.4 mmol/L fasting at home. She has no symptoms of hyperglycaemia. She asks what monitoring she needs going forward.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 76, BP 118/74 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 99%, temperature 36.6°C
data_table.Examination: Body mass index 27.4, no acanthosis nigricans, breastfeeding established
data_table.Investigations: Pregnancy: GDM diagnosed at 28 weeks by 75 g OGTT (fasting 5.4, 1h 11.2, 2h 8.8 mmol/L), diet controlled; postnatal: home fasting glucose 5.6 to 6.4 mmol/L

lead_in: What follow-up plan is most appropriate?

options.text: Repeat 75 g OGTT only at the start of any subsequent pregnancy as routine
options.rationale: Early-pregnancy testing in the next pregnancy is offered, but lifetime monitoring outside pregnancy is also required. (Source: ADIPS postnatal management of women with GDM 2022)
source_refs: ADIPS postnatal management of women with GDM 2022

options.text: Annual fasting plasma glucose only and reassess if she conceives again
options.rationale: Single fasting glucose has lower sensitivity than OGTT or HbA1c and the lifetime risk extends beyond reproductive years. (Source: ADIPS postnatal management of women with GDM 2022)
source_refs: ADIPS postnatal management of women with GDM 2022

options.text: 75 g OGTT at 6-12 weeks postpartum and HbA1c every 1-3 years thereafter
options.rationale: ADIPS recommends a 75 g OGTT at 6-12 weeks postpartum then HbA1c or 75 g OGTT every 1-3 years for life given the elevated lifetime type 2 diabetes risk. (Source: ADIPS postnatal management of women with GDM 2022)
source_refs: ADIPS postnatal management of women with GDM 2022

options.text: Commence metformin 500 mg daily empirically to prevent type 2 diabetes
options.rationale: Empirical metformin without confirmed prediabetes or T2DM is not recommended; lifestyle remains first-line. (Source: ADIPS postnatal management of women with GDM 2022)
source_refs: ADIPS postnatal management of women with GDM 2022

options.text: Refer to endocrinology now for insulin initiation given fasting near 6 mmol/L
options.rationale: Home fasting capillary glucose of 5.6-6.4 mmol/L during established breastfeeding does not by itself indicate insulin; formal venous testing comes first. (Source: ADIPS postnatal management of women with GDM 2022)
source_refs: ADIPS postnatal management of women with GDM 2022

explanation.summary: ADIPS recommends a 75 g oral glucose tolerance test 6-12 weeks postpartum to detect persistent hyperglycaemia, followed by HbA1c (or repeat OGTT) every 1-3 years for life because the lifetime risk of type 2 diabetes is roughly 50-70%. Early-pregnancy testing should also be offered in subsequent pregnancies. Lifestyle remains first-line; empirical metformin is not routine. Home capillary glucose is not diagnostic for diabetes in this context.
key_points: 75 g OGTT at 6-12 weeks postpartum
HbA1c or OGTT every 1-3 years lifelong
Early-pregnancy retesting in next pregnancy
explanation.pearls: GDM is a stress test; about half will develop type 2 diabetes within 10 years - schedule the OGTT before discharge from the postnatal clinic.

sources.label: ADIPS postnatal management of women with GDM 2022

stem: A 16-year-old presents with oligomenorrhoea (cycles every 70-90 days for the last 2 years), mild facial hirsutism (Ferriman-Gallwey 10), and acne unresponsive to topical therapy. She is not sexually active. Body mass index 28.5. Bloods: total testosterone 2.8 nmol/L (0.5-2.4), SHBG 28 nmol/L (30-90), TSH 1.6 mU/L, prolactin 240 mU/L, 17-OH progesterone 4 nmol/L, fasting glucose 5.3 mmol/L. Pelvic ultrasound is reported as showing more than 20 small follicles per ovary. She wants help with cycle regulation and acne. Her mother is concerned about long-term endometrial protection.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 76, BP 118/72 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 99%, temperature 36.7°C
data_table.Examination: BMI 28.5, Ferriman-Gallwey 10, comedonal and inflammatory acne over face and back
data_table.Investigations: Testosterone 2.8 nmol/L, SHBG 28 nmol/L, TSH 1.6 mU/L, prolactin 240 mU/L, 17-OH progesterone 4 nmol/L, fasting glucose 5.3 mmol/L, more than 20 follicles per ovary on ultrasound

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line management?

options.text: Spironolactone 100 mg daily without concurrent contraception cover
options.rationale: Spironolactone is teratogenic and is only used with effective contraception; not appropriate as monotherapy initial step. (Source: International PCOS guideline 2023)

options.text: Metformin 500 mg twice daily as monotherapy for menstrual regulation
options.rationale: Metformin can be added when metabolic features predominate but is not first-line for cycle regulation and hyperandrogenism. (Source: International PCOS guideline 2023)

options.text: Cyclic medroxyprogesterone acetate 10 mg for 10 days every 3 months
options.rationale: Cyclic progestogen provides endometrial protection only and does not address hyperandrogenism. (Source: International PCOS guideline 2023)

options.text: Combined oral contraceptive containing 30 mcg ethinylestradiol with levonorgestrel
options.rationale: International PCOS guideline 2023 recommends combined hormonal contraception first-line for cycle regulation and hyperandrogenism in adolescents; lower-dose preparations are preferred. (Source: International PCOS guideline 2023)

options.text: Letrozole 2.5 mg daily on cycle days 3-7 to induce ovulation each month
options.rationale: Ovulation induction is not the goal in an adolescent not seeking pregnancy. (Source: International PCOS guideline 2023)

explanation.summary: Adolescents with PCOS who seek cycle regulation and management of hyperandrogenism are offered combined hormonal contraception first-line per the 2023 international PCOS guideline, which is endorsed by RANZCOG. Lower-dose formulations are preferred. Lifestyle modification is paired with pharmacotherapy. Metformin is added when metabolic features predominate or after lifestyle alone has failed for impaired glucose tolerance. Spironolactone requires concurrent contraception.
key_points: Combined hormonal contraception first-line for cycle and hyperandrogenism in PCOS
Lifestyle pairs with pharmacotherapy
Spironolactone requires effective contraception due to teratogenicity
explanation.pearls: In adolescents, PCOS diagnosis requires both oligo-anovulation and hyperandrogenism; polycystic ovarian morphology is not used for diagnosis in this age group.

stem: A 29-year-old G3P3 is seen at her six-week postnatal review. Her delivery was an uncomplicated normal vaginal birth. She is fully breastfeeding and her baby has not been formula-supplemented. Her menstrual periods have not returned. She is in a stable relationship and asks about a copper intrauterine device because she does not want hormonal contraception. She has no history of pelvic infection or heavy menstrual bleeding.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 72, BP 116/72 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 99%, temperature 36.7°C
data_table.Examination: Uterus involuted, lochia minimal, no offensive discharge, perineum healed

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Refer to gynaecology theatre for insertion under general anaesthesia next month
options.rationale: Outpatient insertion is appropriate and avoids unnecessary general anaesthesia. (Source: Family Planning Australia contraception guide 2023)

options.text: Defer copper IUD insertion until 12 weeks postpartum to reduce expulsion
options.rationale: Insertion from four weeks postpartum is acceptable; deferring routinely leaves a gap with no contraception. (Source: Family Planning Australia contraception guide 2023)

options.text: Recommend condoms only and review again in six months for the IUD
options.rationale: Condom-only contraception has high typical-use failure rates and the patient has requested LARC. (Source: Family Planning Australia contraception guide 2023)

options.text: Start the progestogen-only pill today and insert copper IUD at six months
options.rationale: She has specifically requested non-hormonal contraception; offering POP overrides her stated preference. (Source: Family Planning Australia contraception guide 2023)

options.text: Insert the copper intrauterine device today after current pregnancy is excluded
options.rationale: Postnatal copper IUD insertion from four weeks postpartum is supported by Faculty of Sexual and Reproductive Health and Family Planning Australia after excluding pregnancy. Fully breastfeeding with amenorrhoea further lowers risk. (Source: Family Planning Australia contraception guide 2023)

explanation.summary: Copper IUD can be inserted immediately postpartum within 48 hours of delivery or any time from four weeks postpartum once the uterus has involuted, after excluding current pregnancy. Lactational amenorrhoea provides additional safety but is not relied upon alone for LARC users. Outpatient insertion at six weeks postnatal review is standard. Honour the patient's request for non-hormonal contraception rather than substituting a hormonal alternative.
key_points: Copper IUD: immediate postpartum (within 48 hours) or from 4 weeks
Exclude pregnancy before insertion
Patient autonomy: honour non-hormonal preference
explanation.pearls: Combining the six-week postnatal review with LARC insertion is the highest-yield contact for unmet contraceptive need.

stem: A 52-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder has taken lithium 1000 mg nightly for 18 years with excellent mood stability. Her eGFR was 78 mL/min/1.73m2 two years ago and is 48 mL/min/1.73m2 today on a confirmatory repeat four weeks after the first reading. She has no acute illness, is well hydrated, and is on no nephrotoxic medications. Her ACR is 0.8 mg/mmol and renal ultrasound shows mildly bulky kidneys without obstruction. Latest lithium level 0.74 mmol/L (target 0.6-0.8 mmol/L). She has had no manic or depressive episodes for 11 years.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 72, BP 132/82 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 99%, temperature 36.6°C
data_table.Examination: Body mass index 26, no peripheral oedema, no tremor
data_table.Investigations: eGFR 48 mL/min/1.73m2 (down from 78 two years ago), ACR 0.8 mg/mmol, lithium level 0.74 mmol/L, TSH 2.4 mU/L, calcium 2.36 mmol/L, renal ultrasound bulky kidneys without obstruction
data_table.Medications: Lithium carbonate 1000 mg nocte for 18 years

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Refer to nephrology and psychiatry to discuss switching from lithium to an alternative
options.rationale: Sustained eGFR decline below 60 mL/min/1.73m2 on confirmatory repeat with otherwise unexplained chronic kidney disease in a long-term lithium user prompts joint nephrology and psychiatry review to discuss switching. RANZCP recommends collaborative decision-making with the patient. (Source: RANZCP mood disorders CPG 2020)

options.text: Reduce lithium dose to 500 mg nightly and recheck eGFR in three months
options.rationale: Lower lithium dose does not reverse established interstitial nephritis and risks relapse if the level falls below the therapeutic window. (Source: RANZCP mood disorders CPG 2020)

options.text: Continue lithium unchanged and recheck eGFR every 12 months as usual care
options.rationale: Confirmed eGFR decline to under 60 mL/min/1.73m2 requires escalation in monitoring frequency and consideration of switch, not routine annual review. (Source: RANZCP mood disorders CPG 2020)

options.text: Stop lithium abruptly today and start sodium valproate 500 mg twice daily
options.rationale: Abrupt lithium cessation carries a high relapse risk and valproate is teratogenic; switching is a planned discussion, not an emergency. (Source: RANZCP mood disorders CPG 2020)

options.text: Add amiloride to protect renal function and continue current lithium dose
options.rationale: Amiloride is used for lithium-induced nephrogenic diabetes insipidus, not for chronic interstitial nephropathy with falling eGFR. (Source: RANZCP mood disorders CPG 2020)

explanation.summary: Long-term lithium causes chronic interstitial nephritis in a minority of users, often slowly progressive after 10-15 years. Sustained eGFR decline below 60 mL/min/1.73m2 (CKD G3a) on confirmatory repeat warrants joint nephrology and psychiatry review. Continuing lithium is sometimes the best option for a patient with no alternative mood stabiliser tolerated, but the conversation must occur. Dose reduction does not reverse established nephropathy. Abrupt cessation carries high relapse risk and is contraindicated.
key_points: Lithium nephropathy: progressive interstitial nephritis over 10+ years
Sustained eGFR under 60 mL/min/1.73m2 triggers nephrology/psychiatry joint review
Lithium cessation, if undertaken, is gradual over months
explanation.pearls: The decision to continue lithium with CKD weighs relapse risk (high) against rate of further renal decline (often modest) - shared decision making with both teams is the standard.

subtopic_detail: Valproate in a woman of reproductive age
stem: A 26-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder has been stable on sodium valproate 1000 mg daily for three years after one severe manic episode. She attends review and tells you she has been in a new relationship for four months and would like to stop her copper IUD and try for a pregnancy in the next year. She has no current symptoms of mania or depression. She was previously intolerant of lithium (severe tremor at therapeutic levels) but has never tried other agents. She has no other medical conditions.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 72, BP 116/74 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 99%, temperature 36.6°C
data_table.Examination: Mental state stable, no thought disorder, mood euthymic, no tremor
data_table.Investigations: Valproate level 380 micromol/L (within therapeutic range), LFTs and FBC normal, beta-hCG negative today

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue valproate during conception and counsel on the 10% major malformation risk
options.rationale: Valproate exposure in early pregnancy carries about 10% major congenital malformation rate and about 30-40% neurodevelopmental impairment. It is contraindicated in pregnancy and in women planning pregnancy unless no alternative. (Source: TGA valproate pregnancy prevention program)

options.text: Switch to lamotrigine before conception with gradual cross-titration and high-dose folate
options.rationale: Lamotrigine is the preferred maintenance mood stabiliser in women planning pregnancy who have not tolerated lithium. Cross-titration with valproate is gradual, contraception is continued until stabilisation, and high-dose folate 5 mg daily is added preconception. (Source: RANZCP mood disorders CPG 2020)

options.text: Switch back to lithium and accept the previous tremor as cosmetic only
options.rationale: Severe tremor at therapeutic level was previously intolerable and is not cosmetic; lamotrigine is the more rational option here. (Source: RANZCP mood disorders CPG 2020)

options.text: Stop all mood stabilisers preconception and restart only if a relapse occurs
options.rationale: Untreated bipolar I disorder relapse rate without prophylaxis is high, particularly during pregnancy and postpartum. (Source: RANZCP mood disorders CPG 2020)

options.text: Continue valproate and add quetiapine 300 mg nightly as additional cover
options.rationale: Adding a second agent does not solve the teratogenicity problem; the issue is valproate itself, not the level of cover. (Source: RANZCP mood disorders CPG 2020)

explanation.summary: Sodium valproate is contraindicated in pregnancy and in women planning pregnancy because of a roughly 10% major malformation rate and a 30-40% rate of neurodevelopmental impairment. The TGA pregnancy prevention program requires effective contraception, annual review, and documented counselling. For bipolar maintenance in women planning pregnancy who do not tolerate lithium, lamotrigine is the preferred switch, cross-titrated gradually with continued contraception until stable, and combined with high-dose folate 5 mg daily preconception.
key_points: Valproate: 10% major malformation rate, 30-40% neurodevelopmental impairment
TGA pregnancy prevention program mandates documented annual review
Lamotrigine is the preferred bipolar maintenance in pregnancy planning
explanation.pearls: Folate 5 mg daily preconception is for women on enzyme-inducing antiepileptics or those at high neural tube defect risk - not just 0.5 mg.

stem: A 27-year-old software engineer has been diagnosed by a psychiatrist with adult ADHD combined presentation. She has childhood symptoms documented in school reports, persistent inattention and impulsivity in adulthood, and significant occupational impairment. She has no history of cardiovascular disease, no substance use disorder, and no psychotic disorder. She has well-controlled generalised anxiety on sertraline 100 mg daily. She has a current Australian driver licence and her resting blood pressure is 124/78 mmHg with pulse 72.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 72, BP 124/78 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 99%, temperature 36.7°C
data_table.Examination: Mental state alert and oriented, no thought disorder, mood mildly anxious but stable
data_table.Investigations: ECG sinus rhythm rate 72, no QT prolongation, no LVH; baseline TFTs normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line pharmacotherapy?

options.text: Methylphenidate immediate release titrated against effect and tolerability
options.rationale: Long-acting preparations are preferred first-line in adult ADHD to reduce diversion and improve adherence; immediate release is reserved for top-up. (Source: Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD 2022)
source_refs: Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD 2022

options.text: Atomoxetine 40 mg daily titrated to 80 mg over four weeks as initial therapy
options.rationale: Atomoxetine is second-line; reserved for stimulant intolerance, contraindication, or substance use disorder concern. (Source: Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD 2022)
source_refs: Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD 2022

options.text: Long-acting lisdexamfetamine started at 30 mg daily and titrated weekly
options.rationale: Long-acting stimulant (lisdexamfetamine or extended-release methylphenidate) is first-line in adult ADHD in Australia. Lisdexamfetamine has a low abuse potential due to lysine prodrug structure. (Source: Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD 2022)
source_refs: Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD 2022

options.text: Guanfacine extended release 1 mg daily titrated to 4 mg over six weeks
options.rationale: Guanfacine is third-line in adults and primarily used in children/adolescents. (Source: Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD 2022)
source_refs: Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD 2022

options.text: Bupropion 150 mg daily titrated to 300 mg over four weeks as monotherapy
options.rationale: Bupropion is not first-line for ADHD; reserved for selected cases or co-existing nicotine dependence. (Source: Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD 2022)
source_refs: Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD 2022

explanation.summary: Long-acting stimulants (lisdexamfetamine or extended-release methylphenidate) are first-line for adult ADHD per the Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline 2022. Lisdexamfetamine is a lysine prodrug, requiring intestinal conversion to active dexamfetamine, which reduces abuse potential. Baseline cardiovascular assessment (history, blood pressure, pulse, ECG when indicated) is required before initiation. Atomoxetine and guanfacine are second/third-line.
key_points: Long-acting stimulant first-line in adult ADHD in Australia
Baseline BP, pulse, and cardiovascular history are required
Atomoxetine reserved for stimulant intolerance or substance use concerns
explanation.pearls: Most state stimulant prescribing rules require a paediatrician or psychiatrist for initiation; GPs continue care under shared care arrangements.

sources.label: Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD 2022

stem: A 22-year-old man with first-episode psychosis is admitted to a psychiatric ward. Over 48 hours he becomes mute, immobile in a wax-flexible posture (he maintains an arm raised when positioned), and resistant to being moved. He has not eaten or drunk for 18 hours and his sodium is 134 mmol/L. He has been receiving risperidone 2 mg daily for four days. He has no fever, normal vitals, no rigidity, no autonomic instability, no rhabdomyolysis. The Bush-Francis Catatonia Rating Scale screen is positive.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 76, BP 122/78 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 99%, temperature 36.7°C
data_table.Examination: Mute, immobile, waxy flexibility, posturing arm maintained when raised, no rigidity, no fever
data_table.Investigations: Sodium 134 mmol/L, creatine kinase 110 U/L (normal), Bush-Francis screen positive
data_table.Medications: Risperidone 2 mg daily commenced four days ago

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Refer urgently for electroconvulsive therapy without prior medication trial
options.rationale: ECT is highly effective in catatonia but is reserved for failure of lorazepam or for malignant catatonia; lorazepam challenge precedes ECT. (Source: RANZCP psychosis CPG 2016)

options.text: Bromocriptine 2.5 mg three times daily with dantrolene for NMS suspicion
options.rationale: There is no fever, no rigidity, and normal creatine kinase. NMS is unlikely and dopamine agonists are not the first move in catatonia without NMS. (Source: RANZCP psychosis CPG 2016)

options.text: Continue risperidone and add diazepam 5 mg orally three times daily
options.rationale: Antipsychotics can worsen catatonia (and may precipitate NMS); the standard is to pause the antipsychotic and use scheduled lorazepam. Oral diazepam is also slower acting than IV/IM lorazepam in this acute context. (Source: RANZCP psychosis CPG 2016)

options.text: Lorazepam 2 mg IV with reassessment of motor signs after 10 minutes
options.rationale: A lorazepam challenge (1-2 mg IV or IM, sometimes followed by a second dose) is both diagnostic and the first-line treatment for catatonia in adults. Response within 5-15 minutes confirms catatonia and supports scheduled benzodiazepine therapy. (Source: RANZCP psychosis CPG 2016)

options.text: Start IV thiamine 300 mg three times daily for suspected Wernicke onset
options.rationale: Although IV thiamine is appropriate in any unwell, malnourished patient (and should be considered here for nutrition), it does not treat catatonia. (Source: RANZCP psychosis CPG 2016)

explanation.summary: Catatonia is recognised on the Bush-Francis Catatonia Rating Scale and includes mutism, immobility, waxy flexibility, and posturing. The lorazepam challenge (1-2 mg IV or IM) is diagnostic and therapeutic; response within minutes supports scheduled benzodiazepine. Antipsychotic medication is paused because of NMS risk. ECT is reserved for lorazepam failure or for malignant catatonia (fever, autonomic instability, rhabdomyolysis). Thiamine is supportive for nutrition but does not treat catatonia.
key_points: Lorazepam challenge 1-2 mg IV/IM is diagnostic and first-line treatment
Pause antipsychotic medication to avoid NMS precipitation
ECT reserved for lorazepam failure or malignant catatonia
explanation.pearls: If response to first lorazepam dose is partial, a second 2 mg dose 30 minutes later is given before declaring failure - catatonia is benzodiazepine-responsive in roughly 70% of adults.

stem: A 28-year-old woman is brought in by ambulance after deliberate ingestion of an unknown quantity of amitriptyline approximately three hours ago. She is drowsy but rousable, GCS 12 (E3V4M5), with dilated pupils and dry skin. She has had one generalised tonic-clonic seizure on arrival lasting 60 seconds that terminated after IV midazolam 5 mg. Pulse rate 124 sinus, blood pressure 92/58 mmHg, respiratory rate 22, SpO2 96% on 6 L Hudson, temperature 37.4°C. Initial ECG shows sinus tachycardia with QRS 132 milliseconds and a terminal R wave in aVR of 4 mm.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 124 sinus, BP 92/58 mmHg, RR 22, SpO2 96% on 6 L Hudson, temperature 37.4°C
data_table.Examination: GCS 12, dilated pupils, dry skin, hyperreflexia, intact gag reflex
data_table.Investigations: ECG QRS 132 ms with terminal R aVR 4 mm; ABG pH 7.30, pCO2 30 mmHg, HCO3 18 mmol/L, lactate 3.2 mmol/L; paracetamol level negative
data_table.Medications: IV midazolam 5 mg given for seizure; IV access x2 with normal saline 500 mL

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: IV activated charcoal 50 g via nasogastric tube as decontamination measure
options.rationale: Charcoal is considered within one hour of ingestion in alert co-operative patients; at three hours with reduced GCS and seizure risk it is not appropriate. (Source: Toxicology Wiki Calvey TCA 2024)

options.text: IV flumazenil 0.2 mg titrated to reversal of central anticholinergic effect
options.rationale: Flumazenil is contraindicated in TCA co-ingestion because it lowers the seizure threshold; she has already seized. (Source: Toxicology Wiki Calvey TCA 2024)

options.text: IV phenytoin 18 mg/kg loading dose to reduce TCA-induced seizure recurrence
options.rationale: Phenytoin is a class IB agent and worsens TCA cardiotoxicity by adding to sodium-channel blockade. Levetiracetam is the preferred second seizure agent if needed. (Source: Toxicology Wiki Calvey TCA 2024)

options.text: IV lipid emulsion 20% 100 mL bolus then infusion at 0.25 mL/kg/min
options.rationale: Lipid emulsion is reserved for refractory cardiotoxicity (cardiac arrest, persistent shock) after sodium bicarbonate; it is not first-line. (Source: Toxicology Wiki Calvey TCA 2024)

options.text: IV sodium bicarbonate 100 mmol bolus targeting QRS narrowing and pH 7.50-7.55
options.rationale: QRS above 100 ms in TCA overdose indicates sodium-channel blockade requiring sodium bicarbonate. Boluses of 1-2 mmol/kg are repeated until QRS narrows below 100 ms and arterial pH reaches 7.50-7.55. (Source: Toxicology Wiki Calvey TCA 2024)

explanation.summary: TCA overdose causes sodium-channel blockade (widened QRS, right axis deviation, terminal R in aVR) plus anticholinergic features. QRS above 100 ms or aVR R-wave above 3 mm triggers IV sodium bicarbonate 1-2 mmol/kg boluses, repeated until QRS narrows below 100 ms and pH reaches 7.50-7.55. Flumazenil is contraindicated. Phenytoin worsens cardiotoxicity. Lipid emulsion is reserved for refractory toxicity. Activated charcoal is only useful within an hour and only if airway is protected.
key_points: QRS above 100 ms in TCA overdose triggers IV sodium bicarbonate 1-2 mmol/kg
Target arterial pH 7.50-7.55 and QRS below 100 ms
Phenytoin contraindicated; flumazenil contraindicated
explanation.pearls: In TCA overdose, a terminal R wave of 3 mm or more in aVR is a sensitive early marker of cardiotoxicity - check aVR before relying on QRS duration.

stem: A 45-year-old man with major depressive disorder (currently in remission on sertraline 100 mg) attends his GP for smoking cessation support. He has smoked 25 cigarettes daily for 22 years and has tried nicotine patches twice with brief success but relapse within three months each time. He is highly motivated to quit, has no cardiovascular disease, and has no recent suicidal ideation. He asks about the strongest pharmacotherapy option available in Australia.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 76, BP 132/82 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 98%, temperature 36.7°C
data_table.Examination: Body mass index 26, no neurological deficit, mental state euthymic
data_table.Investigations: Carbon monoxide breath test 16 ppm, fagerstrom test for nicotine dependence score 8

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line cessation pharmacotherapy?

options.text: Varenicline titrated over the first 7 days and continued for 12 weeks total
options.rationale: Varenicline has higher cessation rates than NRT or bupropion in network meta-analyses. The 2008 EAGLES trial showed no increased neuropsychiatric event rate in stable depression. RACGP supports varenicline first-line in dependent smokers without recent psychiatric instability. (Source: RACGP smoking cessation guidelines 2021)

options.text: Combination NRT patch plus short-acting gum at routine doses for 12 weeks
options.rationale: Combination NRT is reasonable but historically less effective than varenicline in dependent smokers; he has also failed NRT twice. (Source: RACGP smoking cessation guidelines 2021)

options.text: Bupropion 150 mg twice daily commenced 1-2 weeks before quit date
options.rationale: Bupropion is effective but contraindicated in patients with seizure risk and is less effective than varenicline in head-to-head comparisons. (Source: RACGP smoking cessation guidelines 2021)

options.text: Nortriptyline 75 mg nightly as a second-line antidepressant-based option
options.rationale: Nortriptyline is second-line and rarely used now; not first-line for a stable patient who has options. (Source: RACGP smoking cessation guidelines 2021)

options.text: E-cigarettes via prescription pathway as primary pharmacological strategy
options.rationale: Nicotine vaping is now prescription-only in Australia for cessation when first-line therapy fails; not the primary first-line strategy. (Source: TGA nicotine vaping products 2024)

explanation.summary: Varenicline has the highest cessation success rate in head-to-head trials and is first-line for dependent smokers per the RACGP smoking cessation guidelines, including those with stable depression after the EAGLES trial showed no increased neuropsychiatric event rate. Combination NRT is the alternative when varenicline is unavailable or declined. Bupropion is effective but contraindicated in seizure risk. Nicotine vaping products are now prescription-only second-line in Australia.
key_points: Varenicline first-line in dependent smokers including stable depression
Combination NRT alternative first-line option
Nicotine vaping is prescription-only second-line in Australia
explanation.pearls: Set quit date 7-14 days into varenicline titration; nausea is the most common early side effect and improves with taking the dose after food.

stem: A 24-year-old has had three discrete episodes over 18 months. Two episodes featured elevated mood with reduced need for sleep, grandiose delusions, and pressured speech for two weeks each, and resolved on a mood stabiliser. The third episode (most recent) featured persistent auditory hallucinations and persecutory delusions for four weeks WITHOUT any mood symptoms before mood symptoms (low mood, anhedonia, hopelessness) supervened for the next three weeks. She has had no substance use, no thyroid disease, and a normal MRI brain. She is now stable on lithium and risperidone.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 72, BP 118/74 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 99%, temperature 36.7°C
data_table.Examination: Currently euthymic, no thought disorder, no first-rank symptoms today
data_table.Investigations: TSH 1.8 mU/L, urine drug screen negative on multiple occasions, MRI brain normal
data_table.Medications: Lithium 750 mg daily, risperidone 2 mg daily

lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Bipolar I disorder with mood-congruent psychotic features only
options.rationale: Psychosis persisting for at least two weeks without mood symptoms during the same illness distinguishes schizoaffective from bipolar with mood-congruent psychosis. (Source: DSM-5-TR text revision)

options.text: Schizoaffective disorder bipolar type meeting full DSM-5-TR criteria
options.rationale: Schizoaffective disorder requires a major mood episode concurrent with active-phase schizophrenia symptoms PLUS at least 2 weeks of psychosis without prominent mood symptoms during the same lifetime episode. The bipolar subtype applies because manic episodes have occurred. (Source: DSM-5-TR text revision)

options.text: Schizophrenia with co-existing major depressive disorder of mild severity
options.rationale: Two clear manic episodes preclude a schizophrenia plus MDD construction; the affective component meets bipolar criteria. (Source: DSM-5-TR text revision)

options.text: Brief psychotic disorder followed by an unrelated major depressive episode
options.rationale: Brief psychotic disorder lasts less than one month and does not capture the longitudinal pattern with manic and depressive episodes. (Source: DSM-5-TR text revision)

options.text: Substance- or medication-induced psychotic disorder ruled in on history
options.rationale: Repeated negative urine drug screens and an established pattern over 18 months argue against a substance-induced diagnosis. (Source: DSM-5-TR text revision)

explanation.summary: Schizoaffective disorder requires (a) an uninterrupted period of illness during which a major mood episode is concurrent with criterion-A schizophrenia symptoms, (b) at least two weeks of delusions or hallucinations in the absence of a major mood episode during the same lifetime illness, and (c) mood-episode symptoms present for the majority of the active and residual periods. The bipolar subtype applies when manic episodes occur; the depressive subtype applies when only depressive episodes do.
key_points: Schizoaffective: two-week period of psychosis without mood symptoms is the key discriminator from bipolar with psychotic features
Bipolar subtype if any lifetime manic episode
Substance use and primary medical causes must be excluded
explanation.pearls: Most psychiatrists capture the 'two weeks of pure psychosis' criterion with a detailed week-by-week longitudinal chart of the illness, not a single cross-sectional interview.

stem: A 58-year-old man with no prosthetic valve presents with four weeks of fevers, night sweats, and fatigue. He has a known bicuspid aortic valve. Today his blood pressure is 124/68 mmHg, pulse 104, temperature 38.4°C. New early diastolic murmur is heard at the left sternal edge. He has splinter haemorrhages and a small embolic stroke on MRI. Three sets of blood cultures have been collected over an hour. Transthoracic echo shows a 9 mm mobile vegetation on the aortic valve with mild aortic regurgitation. He has no penicillin allergy. The microbiology service has been informed.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 104, BP 124/68 mmHg, RR 18, SpO2 97%, temperature 38.4°C
data_table.Examination: Early diastolic murmur LSE, splinter haemorrhages bilaterally, no Roth spots
data_table.Investigations: Three sets of blood cultures collected; TTE: 9 mm vegetation aortic valve with mild AR; MRI brain: small acute embolic infarct

lead_in: Which empirical antimicrobial regimen is most appropriate while cultures are pending?

options.text: Vancomycin 25-30 mg/kg loading IV with gentamicin 1 mg/kg q8h as adjunct
options.rationale: Empirical vancomycin alone (or with gentamicin) is reserved for severe penicillin allergy or MRSA risk; benzylpenicillin plus flucloxacillin plus gentamicin is the AU first-line. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Ceftriaxone 2 g IV daily monotherapy until cultures speciate the organism
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone monotherapy lacks reliable staphylococcal cover; not first-line empirical. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Benzylpenicillin 1.8 g IV q4h plus flucloxacillin 2 g IV q4h plus gentamicin 1 mg/kg q8h
options.rationale: AU empirical regimen for native-valve infective endocarditis without prosthesis and without severe penicillin allergy covers viridans streptococci, staphylococci, and enterococci pending culture data. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Daptomycin 6 mg/kg daily IV with rifampicin 600 mg orally twice daily
options.rationale: Daptomycin plus rifampicin is a salvage regimen for prosthetic valve MRSA endocarditis; not empirical native valve cover. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Wait for blood culture results before commencing any antibiotic therapy
options.rationale: Delaying antimicrobials in confirmed endocarditis with embolic stroke risk is unsafe; empirical therapy begins immediately after cultures are drawn. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Suspected native-valve infective endocarditis without prosthetic material and without severe penicillin allergy is treated empirically in Australia with benzylpenicillin (viridans streptococci, enterococci) plus flucloxacillin (staphylococci) plus low-dose gentamicin (synergy), pending culture. Severe penicillin allergy or MRSA risk substitutes vancomycin for benzylpenicillin and flucloxacillin. Empirical therapy starts immediately after three sets of blood cultures are drawn.
key_points: AU empirical native valve IE: benzylpenicillin + flucloxacillin + gentamicin
MRSA or severe penicillin allergy: vancomycin substitutes
Antibiotics start immediately after three sets of cultures
explanation.pearls: Synergistic low-dose gentamicin is given for the first 2 weeks then reviewed - renal toxicity is the limiting factor.

stem: A 28-year-old man presents with 10 days of fever, sore throat, generalised rash, and tender cervical and inguinal lymphadenopathy. He had condomless receptive anal sex with a new partner three weeks ago. He has not taken HIV pre-exposure prophylaxis. His Monospot test is negative. Throat swab is negative for group A streptococcus. Bloods show mild lymphopenia, normal LFTs, and CRP 38 mg/L. He is asking specifically about HIV testing.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 96, BP 118/72 mmHg, RR 16, SpO2 99%, temperature 38.4°C
data_table.Examination: Diffuse maculopapular rash on trunk and proximal limbs, tender cervical and inguinal lymphadenopathy, pharyngeal injection
data_table.Investigations: WCC 4.2 x10^9/L (lymphocytes 0.9), CRP 38 mg/L, Monospot negative, throat swab GAS negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Defer testing for 6 weeks given symptoms and repeat fourth-generation assay then
options.rationale: Deferring testing during a clinically suggestive seroconversion illness misses the opportunity for early diagnosis and rapid ART initiation. (Source: ASHM HIV testing policy 2023)

options.text: Third-generation HIV antibody assay alone with repeat at 12 weeks if negative
options.rationale: Third-generation antibody assays have a longer window period; fourth-generation combination plus RNA gives best detection during seroconversion. (Source: ASHM HIV testing policy 2023)

options.text: Western blot confirmation as the initial diagnostic test for clarity of result
options.rationale: Western blot is a confirmatory not a screening test; current AU laboratories use immunoblot or HIV-1/2 differentiation assay as confirmation after reactive screen. (Source: ASHM HIV testing policy 2023)

options.text: Fourth-generation HIV antigen/antibody combination assay plus HIV RNA viral load
options.rationale: Acute seroconversion within 2-4 weeks of exposure is best detected by combined fourth-generation antigen/antibody screen plus HIV RNA PCR; either alone may miss the window. ASHM and CDNA recommend the combination during the window period. (Source: ASHM HIV testing policy 2023)

options.text: CD4 count and lymphocyte subset analysis before any other test is requested
options.rationale: CD4 counts are used after diagnosis for staging; they are not a diagnostic test for acute infection. (Source: ASHM HIV testing policy 2023)

explanation.summary: Acute HIV seroconversion presents as a glandular-fever-like illness with rash and pharyngitis 2-4 weeks after exposure. The diagnostic strategy during the window period combines a fourth-generation antigen/antibody assay (detects p24 antigen at about 15 days post-exposure) with HIV RNA PCR (detects RNA from about 10 days). A reactive screen is confirmed with immunoblot or HIV-1/2 differentiation. Early diagnosis allows immediate ART, partner notification, and PrEP counselling for contacts.
key_points: Window period detection: fourth-generation Ag/Ab combination plus HIV RNA PCR
Acute seroconversion: rash plus pharyngitis plus generalised lymphadenopathy
Confirmatory test is immunoblot or HIV-1/2 differentiation assay
explanation.pearls: Immediate ART initiation in acute seroconversion reduces viral reservoir size and transmissibility - same-day start where possible.

sources.label: Australian Commentary on US DHHS HIV

stem: A 32-year-old healthcare worker has migrated to Australia from a high-TB-incidence country two years ago. Pre-employment screening QuantiFERON-TB Gold Plus is positive (TB1 1.5 IU/mL, TB2 1.8 IU/mL, nil 0.1 IU/mL). She has no fever, weight loss, night sweats, or cough. Chest X-ray is normal.
