She has not had a previous TB diagnosis, is not pregnant, is on no immunosuppression, and her LFTs are normal. She is asking what treatment she needs and why.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 72, BP 118/74 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 99%, temperature 36.7°C
data_table.Examination: No lymphadenopathy, no cough, clear chest, no hepatomegaly
data_table.Investigations: QFT-Plus positive (TB1 1.5, TB2 1.8, nil 0.1 IU/mL); chest X-ray clear; LFTs normal; HIV negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Annual chest X-ray surveillance without antimicrobial therapy
options.rationale: Surveillance does not prevent the 5-10% lifetime reactivation risk; preventive treatment does. (Source: National Tuberculosis Advisory Committee 2023)

options.text: No treatment is needed because the chest X-ray excludes active disease
options.rationale: A normal chest X-ray excludes active disease but does not negate the latent TB diagnosis or the lifetime reactivation risk of 5-10%. (Source: National Tuberculosis Advisory Committee 2023)

options.text: Four drug intensive therapy RIPE for two months followed by RH for four
options.rationale: RIPE is the regimen for active TB, not latent infection. (Source: National Tuberculosis Advisory Committee 2023)

options.text: BCG vaccination repeat dose to prevent reactivation in adulthood
options.rationale: BCG does not prevent reactivation of established latent TB; treatment is the answer. (Source: National Tuberculosis Advisory Committee 2023)

options.text: Isoniazid 300 mg daily plus pyridoxine 25 mg daily for 6-9 months total
options.rationale: Isoniazid plus pyridoxine for 6-9 months is the established Australian first-line latent TB regimen with the most extensive safety data. Three months of weekly rifapentine plus isoniazid (3HP) is an accepted alternative when supervised therapy is available. (Source: National Tuberculosis Advisory Committee 2023)

explanation.summary: Latent TB infection in an Australian healthcare worker from a high-incidence country with positive QFT-Plus and a clear chest X-ray warrants preventive treatment. Isoniazid 300 mg daily plus pyridoxine 25 mg daily for 6-9 months is the established first-line regimen; three months of weekly rifapentine plus isoniazid (3HP) is an accepted alternative with shorter duration. Baseline LFTs and monitoring are required. RIPE is reserved for active disease.
key_points: Latent TB treatment: isoniazid plus pyridoxine 6-9 months
3HP (rifapentine plus isoniazid weekly for 3 months) is alternative
Lifetime reactivation risk of latent TB is 5-10%
explanation.pearls: Pyridoxine is added with isoniazid to prevent peripheral neuropathy, particularly in those with diabetes, alcohol use, malnutrition, or pregnancy.

stem: A 64-year-old man presents with rigors, confusion, and one day of dysuria. Pulse rate 124, blood pressure 84/52 mmHg, respiratory rate 26, SpO2 94% on room air, temperature 39.4°C. Mean arterial pressure is 63 mmHg. Lactate is 4.8 mmol/L. He has received 30 mL/kg of balanced crystalloid over 60 minutes. He has had blood cultures, urine cultures, and piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g IV within the hour. After fluid resuscitation his blood pressure is 92/56 mmHg (MAP 68 mmHg) and his repeat lactate is 4.2 mmol/L.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 116, BP 92/56 mmHg (MAP 68 mmHg) post-fluids, RR 24, SpO2 96% on 4 L NP, temperature 38.6°C
data_table.Examination: Mottled lower limbs, capillary refill 4 seconds, GCS 14
data_table.Investigations: Lactate 4.2 mmol/L (down from 4.8), creatinine 168 micromol/L, WCC 18 x10^9/L, urine culture pending
data_table.Medications: 30 mL/kg balanced crystalloid given; piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g IV given

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next intervention?

options.text: Commence IV noradrenaline infusion via central venous access to target MAP at least 65 mmHg
options.rationale: Persistent hypoperfusion (MAP under 65 mmHg or persistently elevated lactate above 4 mmol/L) after 30 mL/kg crystalloid is the trigger for vasopressors. Noradrenaline is first-line. Surviving Sepsis 2021 supports this within the first hour. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021)

options.text: Administer further 30 mL/kg crystalloid before considering any vasopressor
options.rationale: Beyond 30 mL/kg, further fluid is guided by dynamic assessment; ongoing hypoperfusion needs noradrenaline, not more fluid by default. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021)

options.text: Add vancomycin 25 mg/kg IV empirically without an MRSA risk assessment
options.rationale: Vancomycin is added when MRSA risk is present (recent hospitalisation, prior MRSA, healthcare exposure); not all septic shock patients need it. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021)

options.text: Give IV hydrocortisone 50 mg six hourly before any vasopressor is started
options.rationale: Hydrocortisone is added when vasopressor requirement persists (often greater than 0.25 mcg/kg/min noradrenaline); not before vasopressors. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021)

options.text: Transfuse one unit of packed red blood cells targeting haemoglobin at 100 g/L
options.rationale: Transfusion threshold in septic shock is haemoglobin under 70 g/L in most patients; not 100 g/L. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021)

explanation.summary: Surviving Sepsis 2021 defines hour-1 bundle elements as measuring lactate, taking blood cultures, giving broad-spectrum antibiotics, starting 30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension or lactate above 4 mmol/L, and starting vasopressors if MAP remains under 65 mmHg or lactate stays elevated after initial fluid. Noradrenaline is first-line vasopressor. Hydrocortisone is added when vasopressor requirement persists beyond modest doses. Transfusion targets a haemoglobin of about 70 g/L unless ischaemia is present.
key_points: Vasopressor trigger: MAP under 65 mmHg or lactate above 4 mmol/L after 30 mL/kg crystalloid
Noradrenaline is first-line vasopressor
Hydrocortisone added for ongoing high vasopressor requirement
explanation.pearls: Start vasopressors via peripheral large-bore access if a central line is delayed - do not wait for central access while the patient remains hypoperfused.

subtopic_detail: Necrotising soft tissue infection LRINEC intermediate
stem: A 46-year-old man with type 2 diabetes presents with 24 hours of left thigh pain disproportionate to a small abrasion sustained gardening. The thigh is tense, dusky, and exquisitely tender beyond the visible erythema, with sensory loss over a 6 cm area and crepitus on palpation. Pulse rate 122, blood pressure 102/64 mmHg, respiratory rate 24, SpO2 97% on room air, temperature 38.6°C. Bloods: WCC 16 x10^9/L, CRP 180 mg/L, sodium 132 mmol/L, creatinine 154 micromol/L, glucose 16 mmol/L, haemoglobin 132 g/L. LRINEC score totals 6.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 122, BP 102/64 mmHg, RR 24, SpO2 97%, temperature 38.6°C
data_table.Examination: Left thigh tense dusky tender beyond visible erythema, 6 cm area of sensory loss, crepitus palpable
data_table.Investigations: WCC 16, CRP 180 mg/L, Na 132, creatinine 154 micromol/L, glucose 16 mmol/L, Hb 132 g/L; LRINEC 6 (intermediate)
data_table.Medications: Nil regular; IV access x2 with 500 mL Hartmann's running

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: CT thigh with contrast and admit for observation if no fascial gas is seen
options.rationale: Imaging may help selected stable cases but should NOT delay theatre when bedside hard signs are present; surgical exploration is the gold standard. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Urgent surgical exploration and debridement plus IV meropenem and clindamycin
options.rationale: Pain disproportionate to examination, sensory loss, crepitus, and rapid progression are clinical hard markers of necrotising soft tissue infection that override an intermediate LRINEC score. Theatre exploration is the diagnosis and treatment combined; empirical IV broad-spectrum cover plus clindamycin (toxin suppression) precedes culture data. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Observe for 6 hours with serial LRINEC scoring before deciding on surgery
options.rationale: Delay of even hours significantly increases mortality; bedside hard signs alone justify theatre. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Treat as severe cellulitis with IV flucloxacillin and elevate the limb only
options.rationale: Cellulitis does not produce crepitus, sensory loss, or pain disproportionate; treating as cellulitis misses NF. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Commence hyperbaric oxygen therapy at the regional centre prior to debridement
options.rationale: Hyperbaric oxygen is adjunctive after surgical source control, never a substitute. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Necrotising soft tissue infection presents with pain disproportionate to examination, sensory loss, crepitus, dusky skin, and rapid systemic deterioration. The LRINEC score (>=6 intermediate, >=8 high) is supportive but should not be used to rule out NF in the presence of clinical hard signs. Urgent surgical exploration and debridement is both diagnostic and therapeutic. Empirical antibiotics are broad-spectrum (meropenem or piperacillin-tazobactam) plus clindamycin for exotoxin suppression. Hyperbaric oxygen is adjunctive after source control.
key_points: Hard clinical signs override an intermediate LRINEC
Theatre is diagnosis plus treatment in NF
Empirical cover: broad-spectrum beta-lactam plus clindamycin
explanation.pearls: Pain out of proportion plus crepitus plus mottled skin = call theatre before completing imaging; you can debride a clean thigh and apologise, but cannot un-die the patient.

stem: A 31-year-old woman at 28 weeks gestation presents with 36 hours of fever, myalgia, headache, and dry cough during a confirmed influenza A community outbreak. She has not received the seasonal influenza vaccination this year. Pulse rate 102, blood pressure 116/74 mmHg, respiratory rate 22, SpO2 96% on room air, temperature 38.6°C. She has clear breath sounds and a normal foetal heart rate. Rapid antigen test for influenza A is positive. She has no significant comorbidities.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 102, BP 116/74 mmHg, RR 22, SpO2 96%, temperature 38.6°C, FHR 140
data_table.Examination: Clear breath sounds bilaterally, no chest pain, uterus appropriate for dates
data_table.Investigations: Influenza A RAT positive; chest X-ray not yet performed

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Zanamivir inhaled twice daily preferred over oseltamivir in pregnancy
options.rationale: Oseltamivir is preferred in pregnancy because of systemic absorption needed for severe disease; zanamivir is reserved for resistance. (Source: ATAGI/CDNA influenza in pregnancy 2023)

options.text: Symptomatic management with paracetamol and review in 48 hours
options.rationale: Pregnant women with influenza are at high risk of severe disease and need antiviral therapy promptly. (Source: ATAGI/CDNA influenza in pregnancy 2023)

options.text: Oseltamivir 75 mg twice daily for 5 days commenced today
options.rationale: Pregnancy is an independent risk factor for severe influenza. Oseltamivir is recommended at any gestation as soon as influenza is suspected or confirmed, ideally within 48 hours of symptom onset, but late presentations also benefit. (Source: ATAGI/CDNA influenza in pregnancy 2023)

options.text: IV peramivir as the standard parenteral option for outpatient pregnancy
options.rationale: Peramivir is reserved for inpatients unable to tolerate oral therapy; oseltamivir is first-line outpatient. (Source: ATAGI/CDNA influenza in pregnancy 2023)

options.text: Empirical amoxicillin-clavulanate added to cover bacterial co-infection
options.rationale: Empirical antibiotics are not indicated without evidence of bacterial co-infection (e.g. pneumonia on imaging, raised inflammatory markers, second deterioration). (Source: ATAGI/CDNA influenza in pregnancy 2023)

explanation.summary: Pregnancy is a Category C risk factor for severe influenza and complications. Oseltamivir 75 mg twice daily for 5 days should be started as soon as influenza is suspected or confirmed, with no gestational restriction. Treatment within 48 hours of symptom onset gives the greatest benefit, but late initiation still reduces complications. Influenza vaccine is also recommended at any gestation. Bacterial co-infection is treated only when clinical or imaging evidence supports it.
key_points: Oseltamivir is preferred antiviral in pregnancy at any gestation
Start as soon as influenza suspected, ideally within 48 hours
Influenza vaccine offered at any stage of pregnancy
explanation.pearls: Don't withhold oseltamivir past 48 hours - moderate-to-severe disease in pregnancy still benefits from later start.

stem: A 49-year-old man is referred with a 4.5 cm right adrenal mass detected incidentally on CT for renal colic. He gives a history of episodic palpitations, sweating, and headaches over the last 18 months. His 24-hour urinary fractionated metanephrines are: normetanephrine 8x upper limit of normal, metanephrine 5x upper limit of normal. Plasma metanephrines confirmed elevated. Endocrine surgery has been consulted for laparoscopic adrenalectomy. Current pulse 86 (peaks at 134 during episodes), blood pressure 158/96 mmHg (peaks 220/120).

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 86 baseline (peaks 134), BP 158/96 baseline (peaks 220/120), RR 16, SpO2 99%, temperature 36.7°C
data_table.Examination: Body mass index 26, no orthostatic measurement yet, no other abnormality
data_table.Investigations: 24-hour urinary normetanephrine 8x ULN, metanephrine 5x ULN; plasma metanephrines confirmed elevated; CT abdomen right adrenal mass 4.5 cm

lead_in: What is the most appropriate preoperative pharmacological preparation?

options.text: Start oral amlodipine 5 mg daily as the sole agent prior to surgery
options.rationale: Calcium channel blockers are second-line or adjunctive; alpha blockade is the first step. (Source: Endocrine Society phaeochromocytoma 2014)

options.text: Start oral metoprolol 50 mg twice daily and titrate to heart rate under 90
options.rationale: Beta blockade without prior alpha blockade can precipitate unopposed alpha-mediated hypertensive crisis and is contraindicated. (Source: Endocrine Society phaeochromocytoma 2014)

options.text: Proceed directly to laparoscopic adrenalectomy without any pretreatment
options.rationale: Operating without preoperative blockade carries significant haemodynamic instability and mortality risk. (Source: Endocrine Society phaeochromocytoma 2014)

options.text: Start oral phenoxybenzamine 10 mg twice daily and titrate up over 1-2 weeks
options.rationale: Alpha blockade is started first (10-14 days preoperatively) to prevent intraoperative hypertensive crisis. Phenoxybenzamine is the traditional choice. A beta-blocker is added only after alpha blockade is established. Liberal salt and fluid intake support volume expansion. (Source: Endocrine Society phaeochromocytoma 2014)

options.text: Start IV labetalol infusion for combined alpha and beta blockade
options.rationale: Labetalol has a beta:alpha ratio of about 7:1 oral and 3:1 IV, providing inadequate alpha cover for preoperative phaeochromocytoma preparation. (Source: Endocrine Society phaeochromocytoma 2014)

explanation.summary: Preoperative blockade for phaeochromocytoma starts with alpha antagonism (phenoxybenzamine 10 mg BD escalated, or doxazosin 2-4 mg BD as alternative) for 10-14 days before surgery. Beta blockade is added only after alpha blockade for tachycardia or arrhythmia, never before. Liberal salt and fluid intake corrects volume contraction. Operating without this preparation produces severe intraoperative hypertensive crisis and postoperative hypotension. Calcium channel blockers are adjuncts.
key_points: Alpha blockade always before beta blockade in phaeochromocytoma
Phenoxybenzamine 10-14 days preoperative is traditional first-line
Salt loading and fluid expansion mitigate postoperative hypotension
explanation.pearls: If catecholamine surge is severe perioperatively, IV phentolamine (alpha) and magnesium sulfate are the rescue agents - never beta-block first.

stem: A 44-year-old woman has resistant hypertension on three agents (perindopril 10 mg, amlodipine 10 mg, indapamide 2.5 mg). Her blood pressure today is 162/98 mmHg. She has a serum potassium of 3.1 mmol/L without diuretic-induced losses. She is asymptomatic. She is not pregnant. She has no family history of endocrine disease. She has been off spironolactone, beta-blocker, and direct renin inhibitor for at least four weeks. Her morning aldosterone-renin ratio (ARR) on indapamide is markedly elevated at 65 (cut-off 30 for screen positive).

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 76, BP 162/98 mmHg, RR 16, SpO2 99%, temperature 36.7°C
data_table.Examination: BMI 26, no proximal weakness, no bruit, no abdominal mass
data_table.Investigations: Potassium 3.1 mmol/L, sodium 142 mmol/L, creatinine 76 micromol/L, ARR 65 (cut-off 30 positive); free metanephrines pending
data_table.Medications: Perindopril 10 mg, amlodipine 10 mg, indapamide 2.5 mg daily

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Repeat ARR after 6 weeks off all antihypertensives including amlodipine
options.rationale: ARR has been performed correctly off interfering agents (excluding indapamide is impractical when BP is uncontrolled); confirmation should now proceed. (Source: Endocrine Society primary aldosteronism 2016)

options.text: Proceed directly to CT adrenal protocol without confirmatory suppression test
options.rationale: CT before confirmation risks chasing an incidentaloma in a patient who has essential hypertension and a false-positive ARR. (Source: Endocrine Society primary aldosteronism 2016)

options.text: Adrenal vein sampling now for lateralisation before any other confirmation
options.rationale: AVS is reserved for confirmed primary aldosteronism in surgical candidates after CT; not the next step. (Source: Endocrine Society primary aldosteronism 2016)

options.text: Empirical spironolactone 25 mg daily with no further investigation
options.rationale: Empirical mineralocorticoid antagonism misses surgically curable adenomas in confirmed primary aldosteronism. (Source: Endocrine Society primary aldosteronism 2016)

options.text: Saline infusion suppression test or oral salt loading with 24-hour urinary aldosterone
options.rationale: A positive ARR is a screening test only; confirmation of primary aldosteronism requires a suppression test (saline infusion, fludrocortisone suppression, or oral sodium loading). Confirmation precedes subtype imaging and adrenal vein sampling. (Source: Endocrine Society primary aldosteronism 2016)

explanation.summary: A markedly elevated aldosterone-renin ratio is a screening result, not a diagnosis. Confirmation of primary aldosteronism requires non-suppression of aldosterone after a salt load (intravenous saline 2 L over 4 hours, fludrocortisone suppression, or oral sodium loading with 24-hour urinary aldosterone). Once confirmed, CT adrenal looks for adenoma versus hyperplasia, and adrenal vein sampling lateralises in surgical candidates. Empirical mineralocorticoid antagonism is reserved for non-surgical candidates.
key_points: ARR is screen only; suppression test confirms primary aldosteronism
CT and AVS follow confirmation
Beta-blockers and MRAs interfere with ARR; ACEi and CCB are acceptable
explanation.pearls: Hypokalaemia is present in only about a third of primary aldosteronism cases - normokalaemia does not exclude it.
